description: An object is 'tumoa' when its weight requires substantial force to move or support in context. An obligation or piece of work may also be heavy when someone experiences it as burdensome. 'pamolu' names physical weight before that judgement, while 'tupeka' names a burden borne by a living being.
articulatory_notes: The tongue tip closes against the upper teeth for dental 't' and releases into rounded high-back 'u'. The lips close for nasal 'm' and reopen into rounded mid-back 'o'. Stress falls on 'mo'; final open central 'a' forms a separate unstressed syllable.
sound_symbolism: Rounded 'u' and 'o' keep the middle of 'tumoa' drawn back before the final open 'a' lets the word settle. That low, three-syllable contour can help a speaker remember weight.
usage_notes: The phrase 'tumoa rolutha' describes a heavy wagon, while 'tumoa naseru' extends physical weight to obligations. Neutral comparison or measurement takes 'pamolu'. 'takori' (load) names technical demand on a system, and 'ritako' (workload) gathers work over an interval.

semantic_domains.activity: work or obligation experienced as burdensome through metaphorical weight
semantic_domains.physical: weight that requires substantial force to move or support
